summary: Gruber reacts to Apple's announcement of a Self Service Repair program with skepticism about its significance. He argues that very few people actually want to or are competent enough to repair their own devices, and that the program doesn't change Apple's requirement for genuine parts. He sees the announcement primarily as a political move to reduce regulatory pressure from right-to-repair legislators, rather than a meaningful shift in Apple's repair philosophy. He pushes back on coverage framing the program as a big deal or eco-conscious, noting the cost savings are modest and the program doesn't enable repairs that weren't already possible through authorized channels.
key_points: Almost no one actually wants to or is capable of repairing their own iPhone display or MacBook keyboard, making the practical impact minimal
The program doesn't change Apple's requirement for genuine parts in all authorized repairs, so it's really just about who performs the labor
Gruber sees this primarily as a political move to deflect right-to-repair legislative pressure, not a genuine philosophical shift
The cost savings are modest — roughly $46 for a screen replacement — and require doing skilled labor yourself without specialized tools
Apple's long device support (e.g., iPhone X running iOS 15 well after four years) undermines claims of planned obsolescence
money_quotes: Almost no one wants to repair their own cracked iPhone display or broken MacBook keyboard; even fewer people are actually competent enough to do so.
I don't see how this Self Repair Program really changes anything other than who's performing the labor.

Today's announcement, to my eyes, is about nothing more than reducing regulatory pressure from legislators who've fallen for the false notion that Apple's repair policies, to date, have been driven by profit motive.
Good luck replacing that screen yourself, without any specialized tooling.
We're better off with this Self Service Repair program in place than we were without it.
But to me it clearly seems to be a small deal, not a 'big deal', as Chen claims.
No company comes close to Apple in supporting older devices for longer.
key_insight: Apple's Self Service Repair program is less a victory for right-to-repair and more a savvy political maneuver to defuse legislative pressure, since it changes who performs repairs but not the fundamental economics or parts requirements.
